title: What Is Deployment?
intro: Your task manager works on your laptop — now it's time to put it on the internet so anyone can use it. Let's explore what deployment actually means.

teach.title: From Localhost to the World
teach.body: Deployment is the process of taking your app from 'works on my machine' to 'works for everyone on the internet.' It involves uploading your code to a server, setting up a database, configuring a domain name, and making sure everything runs reliably 24/7.

teach.title: Deployment Is Like Opening a Restaurant
teach.body: Cooking at home is localhost — it works, but only you eat. Deployment is opening a restaurant: you need a location (server), a sign (domain name), health inspections (security), and the ability to serve many people at once (scalability). The recipe is the same, but the logistics are completely different.
Modern platforms like Vercel and Netlify handle most deployment complexity for you. You push code, they build and serve it. Focus on what makes your app special, not server configuration.

cards.content: Modern deployment platforms can automatically deploy your app when you push to the main branch
cards.explanation: This is called continuous deployment. Platforms like Vercel watch your Git repo and deploy automatically on push, so shipping updates is as easy as merging a pull request.

cards.content: You need to buy and manage your own physical server to deploy a web app
cards.explanation: Cloud platforms and serverless providers handle infrastructure for you. Managing physical servers is complex, expensive, and unnecessary for most apps.

cards.content: Deploy to a staging environment first to test changes before they reach real users
cards.explanation: A staging environment mirrors production without affecting real users. It catches issues like broken environment variables or API misconfigurations before they reach your users.

cards.content: It's fine to deploy directly from your laptop to production using FTP
cards.explanation: FTP deployments are manual, error-prone, and bypass CI/CD checks. Use Git-based deployments so changes are tracked, reviewed, and deployed consistently.

cards.content: Preview deployments let you test every pull request on a real URL before merging
cards.explanation: Preview deployments are one of the most useful modern features. Each PR gets its own URL so teammates can review the actual running app, not just the code.

cards.content: Once your app is deployed, you're done — no need to monitor or maintain it
cards.explanation: Deployment is the beginning, not the end. You need to monitor for errors, performance issues, and security vulnerabilities. Apps require ongoing maintenance.
